feedback.text: Dear Sir, I would like to ask for the inclusion in the Digital Justice Act of protection against disabling them from playing purchased video games by their publishers. This problem is becoming increasingly common in the video game industry, resulting in the European Citizens’ Initiative “Stop Destroying Videogames”, which has now been signed by more than 1.4 million European citizens in the EU. This is a problem where a game publisher, e.g. Ubisoft turns off the game, e.g. Crew, for which customers have paid for. The game did not have any end-of-service date and it was not specified during the purchase that the game would be immoveable one day. Although the game has content for one player, Ubisoft has switched off the servers of that game and, as a result, it is not possible to play it again, including content for one player who should not require an internet connection or a central server. Ubisoft had the possibility to issue patches that would allow functionality without the internet, but prefer to destroy the game by switching off servers and does not plan to restore functionality. The money has not been refunded to customers. For the Crew 2 and Crew Motorfest games, but patche promised after the successful start of the ‘Stop Destroying Videogames’ initiative, it is therefore entirely in their power to adapt games that have content for one player but still require the internet and a central server with the publisher, so that these requirements are not necessary and the game, at least in limited capacity, is still accessible and playable. Although one major publisher promised for a few of the selected titles, such repairs were promised, but another large EA publisher is about to destroy Anthem, for which customers also paid and did not know at the time of purchase that the game would not be accessible one day, even though it had content for one player. This is an increasing problem where the publisher gets money for a product that you can hold physically in your hands, but prevents you from using it only because it can but the money will remain. This is not justice. I therefore reiterate my call for a point to be added to the Digital Justice Act against the destruction of purchased video games by their publishers. Thank you.
